Brewer’s book challenges conventional scholarship on the history of the British Crown’s endorsement of slavery in the colonies and enrichment from it. Within that framing, she reexamines Jefferson’s message as he drafted the opening lines of the Declaration.

“When I really looked at them and looked at the way the original first draft was echoed in the deleted clauses,” she said, “I realized there’s no way you can read the Declaration of Independence as not including, at least for Jefferson and the original committee of five people, Black people. They absolutely thought 100% that the words at the beginning about ‘all men are created equal’ included Black people.”

Jefferson’s own words show him condemning slavery and the slave trade in moral terms while still holding deeply racist views of the enslaved. In "Notes on the State of Virginia," he described slavery as a corrupting institution in which “the whole commerce between master and slave is a perpetual exercise of the most boisterous passions, the most unremitting despotism on the one part, and degrading submissions on the other.

“Indeed I tremble for my country when I reflect that God is just: that his justice cannot sleep for ever,” Jefferson wrote.

In the book‑lined study of her Maryland home, Brewer reads aloud from Jefferson’s removed antislavery paragraph: “He has waged cruel war against human nature itself, violating its most sacred rights of life and liberty … determined to keep open a market where MEN should be bought and sold.”

“Every single time he wrote the word MEN,” she said, “he wrote it in all caps and in italics.” In six handwritten draft copies of the Declaration that Jefferson kept for himself, she found the same pattern, capitalizing MEN when proclaiming equality and when describing MEN in bondage. “It’s really striking.”

Her conclusion is not that Jefferson was free of contradiction. He held people in bondage even as he wrote this sentence. It is that the founding argument cannot be separated from slavery, and that the scandal lies less in the absence of principle than in the refusal to follow it where it led.

The words outran the founders

Joseph Ellis, the Pulitzer Prize-winning historian of the founding generation, sees in that tension the nation’s central contradiction.

From his Vermont home, on a mountain crossed by the Continental Army, Ellis drew a sharp point: “How could a government justified by ‘all men are created equal’ acquiesce in the institutionalization of slavery? That’s the great contradiction at the heart of the American founding.”

The Declaration announced equality and consent as self-evident truths while the emerging republic preserved slavery and opened the way for Native dispossession. For Ellis, those are not historical footnotes but structural failures within the founding itself.

In his most recent book, "The Great Contradiction: The Tragic Side of the American Founding," he draws a distinction between what he calls the “Shakespearean” tragedy of slavery, a catastrophe that might have gone another way, and the “Greek” tragedy of Indian removal, rooted in settler hunger for land and thus far harder to avert.

Yet Ellis, like Brewer, does not treat the Declaration’s second sentence as empty. In his account, Jefferson’s words established a standard the nation could not meet but also could not escape. They became a “standard maxim,” available not only to the founders but to later generations who would use the sentence as an indictment of the republic’s failures.

“The words,” he said, “create expectations that subsequent generations will hold you to. They haunt you.” That helps explain why the Declaration keeps resurfacing in every major American struggle over membership and rights.

The Rev. Byron Williams, author of "The Radical Declaration: An Enlightened American Idea," makes a related point from a broader historical angle. What was radical about the Declaration, he argued, was not that it solved the problem of equality, but that it made a universal claim in a world organized by kings, empires and inherited status.

Once political authority is said to derive from the people, and once people are said to be equal in rights, systems of domination acquire a legitimacy problem they cannot fully answer.

Williams is not especially interested in rescuing Jefferson’s reputation. “I’m old enough to remember when we said things like ‘all mankind,’ meaning everyone. So ‘men,’ ‘mankind,’ was this ubiquitous term for everybody,” he said. “Now, what (Jefferson) meant, and given the practice of the founders, they meant men, and we can narrow that down. They meant white male property owners. That’s what they meant. That’s what they had in mind. Here’s the problem: they should have put that in the document. Because they didn’t, I don’t care what they meant.”

Jefferson’s greatest sentence belongs to everyone who can hear themselves in it, said Williams, sitting inside Boston’s Old State House in a room where colonial legislators met under a royal governor’s eye.

Its power lies less in the purity of its author than in the way later generations, at home and abroad, have used its universal language to demand more from their own governments.

How the Declaration sounded in 1776

Americans did not first encounter “all men are created equal” in civics textbooks. They heard it in public, amid war and uncertainty, and they interpreted it through their own circumstances.

For white Patriots, it often meant an assault on monarchy, inherited rank and imperial subordination, an assertion that they were equal as Britons and would be equal as citizens of a new republic.

In her new book, "When the Declaration of Independence Was News, "founding-era historian Emily Sneff brings readers back to the summer when the words first entered public life. With Independence Hall in the background as she sat for an interview, she described the Declaration not as a sacred artifact but as breaking news.

Printed as a broadside, and published in every major colonial newspaper, it was read aloud in city squares and cheered with bell‑ringing. In New York, with Gen. George Washington presiding, a reading was followed by patriots pulling down a statue of George III.

After the Continental Congress approved the Declaration on July 4, 1776, the text moved through the colonies as a bare-bones broadside without signatures attached. Printers rushed out copies that emphasized speed over ceremony, often naming only John Hancock or Congress.

In the months that followed, local readings, reprints and manuscript copies spread the ideas of independence, but there was still no widely circulated, official edition that tied those words to a full, authoritative list of who had signed the document.

In January 1777, the Goddard broadside marked a pivotal moment in the life of the Declaration as the first “official” printing that names most of the men who signed it.

Unlike earlier versions, Goddard’s printing turns a revolutionary manifesto into a document of personal accountability, inviting readers to see independence as a risk borne by identifiable individuals rather than a faceless Congress.

For Sneff, it’s important to remember that this milestone in America’s paper trail of equality and consent was produced by a woman. Mary Katharine Goddard, a Baltimore printer and postmaster, not only oversaw the job but printed her full name at the bottom of the broadside that carried all those male signatures.

In a world where women could not vote and had no formal role in Congress, Sneff sees Goddard’s choice as both a professional claim and a quiet act of defiance: The first “official” edition to list the signers’ names was also the only one in which a woman publicly took responsibility for putting the Declaration, and its roster of ‘treasonous’ signatures, into print.

Black Americans heard a challenge in the promise

Americans cannot rediscover the Declaration honestly without asking how different communities heard the same sentence in radically unequal circumstances, said Noelle Trent, president and CEO of the Museum of African American History in Boston and Nantucket. Trent also oversees the African Meeting House and the AI Douglass installation.

Her work in Boston forces the question of equality into the foreground with the exhibit “Black Voices of the Revolution: Liberty, Emancipation, and the Struggle for Freedom.”

Black Bostonians were not peripheral to the Declaration’s meaning. They were among its earliest and most searching interpreters, she said, listening as white Patriots talked of liberty and asking whether those words applied to them at all.

“Well, I think that’s the goal of this exhibition, to enhance everyone’s understanding,” said Trent. “You may know about the Boston Tea Party. You might know about Paul Revere’s ride. And this is just another aspect of that story. Our goal is that we help round out people’s vision of what the struggle was and how this great American experiment got started.”

For enslaved and free Black Americans, “all men are created equal” did not arrive as a settled truth. It arrived as a challenge. Does that mean me? Does that mean my family? Does it mean freedom from bondage, or only freedom for others to govern themselves? The question is not abstract, she said, it is visceral.

Phillis Wheatley Peters, America’s first published Black poet, is one emblem of that complicated patriotism. Wheatley’s poems in support of Washington and the Revolutionary cause are often cited as evidence of Black loyalty to the Patriot side. But they also reflect “a greater intellectual capacity of the Black community in Boston at the time, both enslaved and free,” Trent said.

On Boston’s wharves and in its taverns and markets, an international city’s arguments about taxes, tariffs, war and the future of the colonies spilled into everyday talk. Black Bostonians were part of those debates, weighing which side, Patriot or Loyalist, “held the greatest promise for their future” and for their children, she said.

Boston’s African Meeting House was built in 1806. In the 1830s, it housed the Massachusetts General Colored Association, one of the earliest Black abolitionist groups, and the New England Anti‑Slavery Society founded by William Lloyd Garrison.

“If you were someone of significance to this movement,” Trent said, “odds are you came through Boston and you spoke at the African Meeting House.”

Douglass himself spoke there on a number of occasions as his thinking evolved “from a moral‑suasion abolitionism to a more political type of abolitionism,” she said.

That history makes the AI Douglass feel less like a gimmick and more like continuity: a new way for visitors to test the Declaration’s words against the witness of someone who spent his life prosecuting its failures and claiming its promises.

‘The Great Contradiction’

At the top of the Declaration, Jefferson gave the world a vocabulary for liberty and equality that still shapes how Americans understand themselves.

Near the end of the document, he added a grievance that has marked Indigenous communities for generations, denouncing “merciless Indian savages, whose known rule of warfare is the undistinguished destruction of all ages, sexes and conditions.”

For Donald Grinde Jr., a member of the Yamasee tribe and professor emeritus of history at the University at Buffalo, that passage is “a typical statement of the racism toward Native people” that was present in colonial society.

Jefferson and Benjamin Franklin knew there was a market for interest in these societies, he said. Franklin “becomes the equivalent of a billionaire for selling books about Indians” and Jefferson devotes many passages of his "Notes on the State of Virginia" to Native life.

But the frontier was sold as a promise: “Move out there, get a farm, you can raise your children,” Grinde said. “So, Indians have to get out of the way and stop resisting white settlement.”

Jefferson’s final grievance against the British Crown, grievance 27 in the Declaration, gave voice to institutional racism that “informed Indigenous–U.S. relationships for the next century, century and a half” by helping Americans see Native people as a threat to their well‑being and as “vagrants on land that they regarded as being justly theirs,” said David Martínez, an Akimel O’odham member of the Gila River Indian Community.

That perception translated into “a raft of policies that devastated Indian Country from one end of the continent to the other,” said Martínez, professor of American studies and transborder studies at Arizona State University.

It led to “the rounding up of Indigenous people onto the reservation system against their will,” with lives lost, families separated and trauma that “has been influencing the Indigenous community ever since.”

"The Great Contradiction," as Ellis titled his latest book, is that Native political thought and practice shaped the very ideas that the Declaration and the U.S. Constitution, signed in 1787, celebrated.

Long before 1776, the Haudenosaunee, or Iroquois Confederacy, “had been interacting with people to negotiate treaties for several centuries,” said Grinde, coming to major cities “particularly Philadelphia” to explain “how our government works.”

At the 1754 Albany Congress, colonial delegates invited the Iroquois and listened as Franklin outlined a plan of union modeled in part on their example: a confederacy in which the confederacy does the foreign policy, tribes handle their own affairs, and “even on the local level” villages decide matters like marriage, divorce and custody, Grinde said.

Native societies were living out principles of liberty and equality long before those words appeared in Jefferson’s draft. Grinde describes Indigenous communities with “no nobility,” where “everybody had a say” and “you were all part of a community, and there was no one better than you.”

Leaders were chosen and could be removed: among the Iroquois, “if the clan mothers didn’t like what they were doing,” they could depose chiefs, he said. War leaders were selected by the men who would follow them into battle and replaced when necessary.

For Martínez, reading the Declaration as an Indigenous historian underscores a shared “inherent desire to be free, to live without the burden of another person or another government or another entity oppressing me as a person, as a community member,” a value he calls “universal.”

A 21st‑century commitment to both liberty and equality, Martínez suggests, requires holding two paradoxical facts together: that Native ideas helped shape an American vision of union, and that Native nations were simultaneously cast as enemies to be removed or contained.

He encouraged reflection during this anniversary year. It’s “a little disingenuous to talk about ideals without being candid about the harm behind them...(but) it is important to try to resolve our conflicts so that we’re not living in a constant state of anger and conflict, because no one stands to benefit by keeping a historic grudge and trying to live a life of revenge either.”

Sitting for an interview on ASU’s Tempe campus, Martínez embraced hope for the future but only if it’s rooted in truth about the past.

“There’s always an opportunity to build relationships and to make peace with our history,” he said. “But… you can’t really mend a relationship unless you’re honest about what you’re mending, what wound it is that you’re trying to heal.”

Compromise, propaganda and the limits of equality

When Jefferson wrote “all men are created equal,” the exclusions were obvious to those in the room, said Notre Dame history professor Felipe Fernández‑Armesto. “Well, obviously, Blacks were excluded.”

He challenged scholarship about Jefferson’s intent that stretches the imagination beyond the realities of the time he lived.

“I don’t think Jefferson remotely imagined himself as a prophet,” said Fernández‑Armesto, an award-winning author of more than 20 books, including "Our America: A Hispanic History of the United States." “They were trying to hold on to their power, to their status, to their wealth and to the possibility of creating their own empire in the United States. I think that’s what really mattered.”

If Brewer insists that Jefferson’s greatest sentence asserts a universal principle, and Ellis that it set a standard the nation could not escape, Fernández‑Armesto offers a third truth: that it emerged from compromise and self-interest.

The thing “you’ve got to understand about the Declaration is that it’s also a work of propaganda,” he said. “It was there to justify the American Revolution before the world, quite a hostile world that wasn’t very keen on republics, wasn’t very keen on revolutions...(and) the way to succeed with propaganda or advertising of any sort is to use a lot of good words. You don’t worry too much about what they really mean, whether they correspond to any truthful reality about what it is you’re trying to sell.”

Any reinterpretation of Jefferson’s intent must consider that while he wrote his draft in an apartment on Market Street in Philadelphia, he was groomed each day by his enslaved body servant, Robert Hemings, said Zara Anishanslin, author of "The Painter's Fire. A Forgotten History of the Artists Who Championed the American Revolution."

Hemings was the half-brother of Jefferson's wife, Martha. Robert’s sister, Martha’s half-sister, was Sally Hemings, who at the age of 14 traveled with Jefferson to Paris as the enslaved servant to Jefferson and Martha’s daughter Maria. Sally gave birth to at least six of Jefferson’s children.

Jefferson “is a perfect example of the contradiction at the heart not just of himself, but at the heart of the Declaration,” said Anishanslin, an associate professor of history and art history at the University of Delaware.

“Most people, even though the founders were writing about them as universal rights of man, did not believe that they extended in the same way to women or to enslaved people,” she said. “There are radical thinkers — white radical male thinkers — and there are certainly plenty of women and enslaved people who themselves are thinking more expansively about liberty.”

Over the course of 250 years, those implicit rights do get extended to the emancipation of enslaved people, women getting the right to vote, and the Civil Rights Act, but not without a fight.

“It takes a long time in some cases. But I think that promise is there,” said Anishanslin. “And I think that’s one reason that the Declaration holds that power, and that’s why people hear the words of that first paragraph and respond to it so deeply.”

What the sentence asks of us at 250

When we talk about equality in 2026, the word can cover everything from voting rights to economic opportunity and who gets a meaningful say in public life.

For Timothy Sandefur, vice president for legal affairs at the Goldwater Institute, questions about equality begin with what the Declaration actually means in law and whether its promise that “all men are created equal” still binds courts and legislatures.

Standing by a replica of the Liberty Bell on Arizona’s Capitol Mall, Sandefur laments that the natural‑rights ideas the founders took for granted, ideas that ground equality in “the laws of nature and of nature’s God,” are today “largely ridiculed and rejected by the overwhelming majority of the legal profession.”

“There are a number of leading legal intellectuals (including) Antonin Scalia and Amy Coney Barrett who say that the Declaration is not law at all, that it’s merely political rhetoric,” said Sandefur, author of the just released "Proclaiming Liberty: John Adams, Thomas Jefferson and the Declaration of Independence."

“That’s completely wrong,” he said. “In fact, the Declaration of Independence is law. It’s part of our Constitution. It appears in volume one, page one of the Statutes at Large of the United States, and in the first volume of the United States Code... (and) it’s been incorporated by reference into every state constitution since the Civil War.”

He traces today’s legal skepticism to early‑20th‑century progressive thinkers like Oliver Wendell Holmes, Felix Frankfurter and John Dewey, who argued that “everything about justice and rights and politics and the law; those are all social constructs” that can be altered to suit “the needs of the day.”

If rights and equality are treated merely as adjustable policy preferences, Sandefur warns, then “they aren’t actually rights to begin with,” and minorities who depend on those principles for protection are left at the mercy of shifting majorities.

Sandefur points to Abraham Lincoln’s famous image of the Declaration as “apples of gold in pictures of silver,” with the Constitution’s purpose being to “highlight and bring to fruition the Declaration’s principle of equal liberty.”

Looking toward the Declaration’s anniversary, Sandefur quotes James Madison: “America has set the example of charters of power granted by liberty” and flips a familiar metaphor about government on its head.

“We often in today’s world speak of government as if it’s our parent,” he said. But for the revolutionaries, “government is our child,” created by people who are “fundamentally free” and who delegate power to their institutions.

At 250, the question is no longer what Jefferson meant. It is whether Americans are willing to mean the words more fully now than the country did then.

In Boston, the AI Douglass reminds visitors that the task is the same as it was in his own century: to call out the hypocrisy with one hand and hold on to the saving principle with the other.

To celebrate the Declaration, Trent urged parents to read the Declaration with their children and to tell them America has always been an experiment.

Quoting Coretta Scott King, she reminds us that “freedom isn’t free; you have to earn it with every generation.” Each generation has to decide “what we’re willing to stand for in this moment.”

That is what makes the AI Douglass encounter so potent. Douglass may be the greatest reader the Declaration ever produced because he refused both easy reverence and easy dismissal.

Trent, Williams and Sandefur are united on this final point: Douglass condemned the nation’s hypocrisy in blistering terms yet still called the Declaration’s ideals “saving principles.”

He understood that the sentence’s author had failed it, but he also understood that the words could be seized and redirected by those excluded from their first circle of meaning.

Greg Burton is the executive editor of The Arizona Republic.

To read the entire series of Outspoken interviews and learn what the nation’s leading historians are thinking about the Declaration of Independence, and to see all of our coverage of USA250, go tousatoday.com/usa250/outspoken-history/.

search_result.title: King Charles looks to royal visit to mend rift with Trump - USA Today
search_result.text:
April 25, 2026, 3:00 a.m. ET

WASHINGTON – King Charles III’s mother, Queen Elizabeth II, knew how to finesse her way through an awkward situation.

Faced with toasting the United Kingdom's former colonies on the occasion of their 200th birthday, the queen stood alongside President Gerald Ford beneath a big tent over the White House Rose Garden on the night of July 7, 1976, and graciously raised a glass to America and all that it had become since declaring its independence from Great Britain.

“After all,” she said, “nobody can say that what happened on the Fourth of July, 1776, was not very much a bilateral affair between us.”

(Cue the laughter, please.)

Fifty years later, another British monarch – King Charles, accompanied by his wife, Queen Camilla – is returning to these shores to commemorate America’s 250th birthday. But unlike his mother’s visit, which came during a relatively calm period after transatlantic tensions over the Vietnam War, Charles’ sojourn to the states comes amid the most serious rift in decades between the White House and the British government.

President Donald Trump has sparred with, insulted and, on occasion, even mocked Prime Minister Keir Starmer over tariffs (a cornerstone of Trump’s economic policy); the future of the NATO alliance (Trump questions its value); the war in Iran (the United Kingdom wants no role in it); free speech (the White House claims Britain is censoring conservative views); Greenland (Trump wants it); and Canada (Trump says it should become the 51st state).

"This is not Winston Churchill we're dealing with,” Trump said of Starmer in front of a group of reporters in the Oval Office on March 3.

Against that backdrop comes Charles, who arrives in Washington on April 27, with royal pomp and pageantry for a four-day state visit that includes a formal state dinner at the White House, a rare address to a joint session of Congress and a ceremonial stop at the memorial dedicated to the 9/11 terrorist attacks in New York.

For Britain, Charles’ visit offers a chance to reset the “special relationship” that has bonded it to the United States through two world wars and decades of other perils. For Charles, the trip will offer a chance to prove to the world that he shares his mother’s proficiency in diplomacy.

Asked whether the king’s visit could repair relations with the United States, Trump told the BBC: “Absolutely. He’s fantastic. He’s a fantastic man.”

King Charles 'will find a way to charm Trump'

But how do you charm a leader like Trump, who often speaks to foreign dignitaries like the blunt New Yorker he is instead of in the formal language of diplomatic communications?

“That is actually the big question – how (Charles) is going to approach this,” said Max Bergmann, director of the Europe, Russia and Eurasia Program at the Center for Strategic and International Studies, a nonprofit policy research group in Washington.

Royal watchers say Charles is up to the task. In some ways, he’s better in these kinds of settings than his mother, who sometimes came across as too stiff and formal, said British historian Andrew Lownie, who has written extensively about the royal family.

“He’s as good if not better than his mother,” Lownie said. “He’s much more outgoing in many ways. He’s more charming. He has learned all of the tricks from watching his mother. He will find a way to charm Trump.”

Charles had a lot more time to prepare to become the monarch than his mother, who was just 27 when she ascended the throne upon the death of her father. Charles was 73 when he finally became king after his mother’s death, which meant he had seven decades as a royal apprentice before he finally stepped into the top job.

In Washington, Charles will likely emphasize the common bonds of language and shared history that have been the foundational stones of the long friendship between the United States and Britain, said Matt Beech, director of the Centre for British Politics at the University of Hull.

The United States “is born out of the cultures and traditions and practices of England,” he said. “The language that the founders wrote the Declaration of Independence in is the language of the United States to this day.”

If those commonalities aren’t enough to repair the recent bad blood between the countries, Charles has another trump card at his disposal: The American president seems smitten with the British royal family.

“They gave me a tremendously, the five-star dinner, and it was really incredible,” Trump gushed to Susan Page, Washington bureau chief for USA TODAY and author of the recently published book “The Queen and Her Presidents,” about his state dinner at Buckingham Palace in 2019.

Trump, who says one of his earliest memories was watching television coverage of Queen Elizabeth’s coronation with his Scottish-born mother, told Page he and Elizabeth had “a great chemistry together.”

“I sat with her for hours, and Camilla was on my right and she was on my left, and we talked for a long time,” he said.

The presidents and the queen:: When Queen Elizabeth II phoned Trump and other scoops from a new book

Trump and Charles have met multiple times, most recently last September when the king hosted the president for a second state dinner at Windsor Castle. Trump has spoken highly of Charles, but what Charles thinks of him isn’t as clear. Charles reportedly takes issue with some of Trump’s policies, particularly his stance on climate change, but has not spoken out publicly against them.

“They seem to get on reasonably well in public,” Beech said. “We don't know really about in private. But in public, they do, and that matters.”

'Tortured relationship'

Charles is the latest head of the royal family, but he’s a different leader than his mother. His reign comes in a different era with different challenges.

The relationship between Washington and London has been wilting for years, Bergmann said, starting with the end of the Cold War and the United States putting less emphasis on Russia and Europe. Britain’s strategic significance for the United States eroded further with Brexit, starting in 2016 with the approval of a referendum to leave the European Union and the formal withdrawal four years later.

U.S. leaders had long looked to Britain as an important pro-American voice in the European bloc. But with the United Kingdom no longer an EU member, “the geopolitical reality is that made the U.K. much less important to the United States,” Bergmann said.

With Trump’s return to power last year and his tensions with Starmer, “you have this sort of tortured relationship right now where the U.K. is trying to kind of maintain things, the U.S. is trying to maintain things – sometimes that works, sometimes it doesn’t,” Bergmann said. “But the relationship is just no longer as close as it once was. We’re not going to them for advice, and they’re not coming to us.”

The king's visit: Royal garden party welcoming King Charles to US breaks key tradition

Starmer's problems at home

Starmer may be hoping Trump's fondness for the king will help put him back in the president’s good graces. But that won't help the prime minister at home, where he has faced increasing calls for his resignation over allegations he deliberately misled lawmakers over his appointment of the former British ambassador to the United States, Peter Mandelson.

Mandelson, at the time Starmer gave him Britain's highest-profile overseas diplomatic post, had known ties to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, who died by suicide in a New York prison cell in 2019. Epstein had socialized with several powerful and prominent men in business and politics, including Trump and the king’s brother, Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor, formerly Prince Andrew. Mountbatten-Windsor was stripped of his royal titles last year because of his association with Epstein.

In addition, Starmer's government has been in deep trouble with voters ahead of local elections in May because of an economy facing sluggish growth, persistently high inflation, creaky public services and a national debate over immigration that has been dominated by Reform UK, a party run by Trump ally Nigel Farage.

“Is (Starmer) going to remain as leader?” Bergmann said. “I think there’s some questions about how Charles approaches this.”

Diplomacy and deep ties

As king, Charles is Britain’s head of state but not the head of the government – a distinction that makes the royal family an important asset when it comes to diplomacy and dealing with foreign leaders like Trump.

Charles’ visit is about more than smoothing over troubled waters with one administration, said Richard Whitman, a British professor of politics and international relations at the University of Kent.

It’s a reminder that “the ties are a bit deeper than politicians who come and go,” Whitman said.

And so Charles, like his mother a half-century ago, will stand alongside the U.S. president next week and raise a glass to Britain’s former colonies as they celebrate their independence, their 250th birthday and the world leader they’ve become.

Won’t that be a bit uncomfortable for the British king?

Not at all, Whitman said. The Brits have made their peace with that piece of history.

“Obviously, we still think you made the wrong choice,” he joked. “But that's on you, not us.”

Michael Collins writes about the intersection of politics and culture. A veteran reporter, he has covered the White House and Congress. Follow him on X: @mcollinsNEWS

Contributing from London: Kim Hjelmgaard

search_result.title: Who is the only US president to have served more than 8 years?
search_result.text:
Updated March 31, 2025, 3:28 p.m. ET

President Donald Trump confirmed rumors he could seek an unconstitutional third presidential term, telling NBC News Sunday he was "not joking."

“A lot of people want me to do it,” Trump said in a phone interview. "But, I mean, I basically tell them we have a long way to go, you know, it’s very early in the administration."

Under the 22nd Amendment of the U.S. Constitution, presidents may only be elected to two terms.

Trump teased "methods" he could have to do it, but did not elaborate on any plans, saying he wanted to focus on his current presidency.

Only one other president in U.S. history has served more than two terms. Who was it?

Will TikTok be banned? What to know about Trump's plan to extend the deadline

FDR broke two-term tradition during WW2, 22nd Amendment followed

America's first President George Washington voluntarily stepped down after two terms, creating an unofficial tradition for future presidents to follow suit.

President Franklin D. Roosevelt was the first and only president to break that tradition. The country was still recovering from the Great Depression, and at the dawn of World War II, he was re-elected to his third term. After leading the country through the global war, he was elected again in 1944, but died the following year.

A movement in the House of Representatives to officially limit the presidency terms, now ratified as the 22nd Amendment, began two years after Roosevelt's death.

Can Trump run for a third term?

Under the Constitution as it stands, no.

Changes to the Constitution are extremely difficult and rare, as it requires two-thirds majority vote in both the House and Senate. States can also spur an amendment, but it requires two-thirds of the state legislatures to call a constitutional convention and three-fourths to ratify it.

Trump confirmed one way he could serve another term is if Vice President JD Vance runs for office and then hands the role to Trump, NBC News reported.

"No matter how you look at it, you've got a long time to go," he said to NBC. "We have a long time. We have almost four years to go and that's a long time but despite that so many people are saying you've got to run again."

Contributing: Riley Beggin

Kinsey Crowley is a trending news reporter at USA TODAY. Reach her at kcrowley@gannett.com. Follow her on X and TikTok @kinseycrowley or Bluesky at @kinseycrowley.bsky.social.

search_result.title: 2020 America is still vulnerable to the dangers George Washington ...
search_result.text:
David Huebner

Opinion contributor

Updated Nov. 17, 2020, 9:36 a.m. ET

We Americans traditionally celebrate the birth of our democracy on July 4, commemorating the day in 1776 when the Founders signed the Declaration of Independence. This document was a remarkable assertion of the primacy of the people over their government for the first time in recorded history.

Arguably, though, the United States’ future as a democratic republic was only cemented much later, on March 4, 1797, when John Adams was sworn in as George Washington’s successor. Some Founders, including Alexander Hamilton, did not want to limit presidential tenure, and Washington could have stayed on. Instead, he made a decision to decline a third term and return home to Mount Vernon.

If you have not read Washington’s Farewell Address, in which he declared his intention to step away, you should, particularly at this fraught moment. It’s as if he could see through the centuries to November 2020.

Using power to destroy democracy

He railed against regionalism and partisanship, against “ambitious, corrupted, or deluded citizens,” against those who open the door to foreign influence and “mislead public opinion.” He warned against the rise of “cunning, ambitious, and unprincipled men” who “subvert the power of the people and to usurp for themselves the reins of government, destroying afterwards” the very democratic systems that first put them into office.

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

Above all, he elevated the rule of law and vigilant protection of our constitutional processes, cautioning that “all obstructions to the execution of the laws … with the real design to direct, control, counteract, or awe the regular deliberation and action of the constituted authorities, are destructive” of the people’s prerogative, and indeed are “of fatal tendency.”

He knew where the dangers lurked and was remarkably prescient about their eternal lure. It would be a grave mistake to assume that American democracy has become immune to the dangers of which Washington warned.

It has not. And never will.

My grandparents emigrated from Germany between the world wars. Their generation of old-country family visited regularly when I was a kid. I remember them reminiscing about the gradual collapse of the German republic in the 1920s and 1930s, when a properly-installed chancellor began to urge his torch-bearing partisans into the streets, demonize political opponents, question and then cancel elections, subject science to politics, corrupt courts and attack the press.

They never thought all that would lead where it led. No one ever does.

I have lived a good bit of my career overseas, first as a lawyer in China and elsewhere and then as the American ambassador to New Zealand and Samoa. I have seen my country through the eyes of a dozen other societies. Yes, our innovative economy and popular culture are seductive, but our position in the world and our prosperity at home are rooted in our political norms and government institutions — as George Washington clearly recognized.

Anxiety and faith in unsettled America

It should give us pause when some of our closest friends in the global community publicly call our current political situation “perhaps a bit scary,” as German Foreign Minister Heiko Maas did — or feel compelled to announce that “we have faith in the institutions in the United States,” as recently re-elected Prime Minister Jacinda Ardern of New Zealand has.

Both anxiety and faith are natural reactions to where we are today.

More Americans turned out to vote this month than in any election in American history. With the overwhelming majority of those ballots counted, President-elect Joe Biden has an insurmountable lead of over 5 million votes and a decisive margin in the Electoral College.

Beyond irresponsible: Republicans must put Americans and reality ahead of fealty to Trump

Yet some who profit from subverting the institutions Washington helped create are working overtime to undermine confidence in the vote and invalidate Biden's win. They will not succeed. Over the last four years, they have done much damage to our institutions at home and our reputation abroad, but they have not brought us, the American people, to our knees.

All the votes will be counted. “Inconvenient” votes will not be redefined as “illegal.” A new Congress will be seated. And a new president will soon enter the Oval Office. Messy as it can be, our democracy will function as intended.

In the coming days, we Americans must stay strong and vigilant. We must defend our institutions, whatever our politics. And we must abide by the words of George Washington, perhaps our most selfless and principled president, not those of our least.

David Huebner was U.S. Ambassador to New Zealand from 2009 to 2014. Follow him on Twitter: @DavidHuebner

search_result.title: Who was the first president? List of all the U.S. ... - USA Today
search_result.text:
Jan. 3, 2023 Updated June 28, 2024, 11:00 a.m. ET

To qualify for presidency in the United States, there are three requirements, according to the Constitution:

Must be at least 35 years old

Be a natural born citizen of the U.S.

Must have lived in the U.S. for at least 14 years

There have been 46 presidents throughout U.S. history. With nearly 250 years of history, it can be easy to forget who held office and when. So, here is rundown on the first president, the current and all those in-between.

Who was the first president?

George Washington was the first president of the United States, taking his oath of office on April 30, 1789, according to the White House. Prior to his presidency, Washington was the Commander in Chief of the Continental Army.

Washington retired after two terms, setting a precedent for future presidents. He held the role from 1789 to 1797.

When did George Washington die?

After his retirement, Washington spent his time at Mount Vernon until he died of a throat infection on Dec. 14, 1799, says the White House.

Washington was 67 years old when he died.

How many presidents were there before Washington?

While Washington is recognized as the first president of our nation, there were several "presidents" before him.

During the Continental Congresses and Confederation Congress, a "president" was elected by the delegates to oversee the legislative session, according to the U.S. House of Representatives. This official had many responsibilities including "ruling on parliamentary issues, managing official correspondence, advancing or holding back legislation," among others.

These are the presidents of the Continental Congresses and Confederation Congress and their term lengths:

Peyton Randolph: Sep. 5 – Oct. 22, 1774

Henry Middleton: Oct. 22 – Oct. 26, 1774

Peyton Randolph: May 10 – May 24, 1775

John Hancock: May 24, 1775 – Oct. 31, 1777

Henry Laurens: Nov. 1, 1777 – Dec. 9, 1778

John Jay: Dec. 10, 1778 – Sep. 27, 1779

Samuel Huntington: Sep. 28, 1779 – Mar. 1, 1781

Samuel Huntington: Mar. 2 – July 6, 1781

Thomas McKean: July 10 – Oct. 23, 1781

John Hanson: Nov. 5, 1781 – Nov. 3, 1782

Elias Boudinot: Nov. 4, 1782–Nov. 3, 1783

Thomas Mifflin: Nov. 3, 1783 – Nov. 30, 1784

Richard Henry Lee: Nov. 30, 1784 – Nov. 4, 1785

John Hancock: Nov. 23, 1785 – June 5, 1786

Nathaniel Gorham: June 6, 1786 – Feb. 2, 1787

Arthur St. Clair: Feb. 2 – Oct. 5, 1787

Cyrus Griffin: Jan. 22, 1788 – Mar. 2, 1789

How old do you have to be to be president? What running for the highest office requires.

Who is the president of the United States?

The 46th president of the United States is Joe Biden. Biden previously represented Delaware for 36 years in the Senate, later becoming the 47th vice president of the U.S. under former President Barrack Obama.

Biden assumed office on Jan. 20, 2021 with Kamala Harris as his vice president, making Harris the first female vice president in U.S. history, as well as the first Black American and first Asian American vice president.

Presidents of the United States, in order

Here are all the presidents in U.S. history in order from first to current:

George Washington John Adams Thomas Jefferson James Madison James Monroe John Quincy Adams Andrew Jackson Martin Van Buren William Henry Harrison John Tyler James K. Polk Zachary Taylor Millard Fillmore Franklin Pierce James Buchanan Abraham Lincoln Andrew Johnson Ulysses S. Grant Rutherford B. Hayes James Garfield Chester A. Arthur Grover Cleveland Benjamin Harrison Grover Cleveland William McKinley Theodore Roosevelt William Howard Taft Woodrow Wilson Warren G. Harding Calvin Coolidge Herbert Hoover Franklin D. Roosevelt Harry S. Truman Dwight D. Eisenhower John F. Kennedy Lyndon B. Johnson Richard M. Nixon Gerald R. Ford Jimmy Carter Ronald Reagan George H. W. Bush Bill Clinton George W. Bush Barack Obama Donald Trump Joe Biden

Just Curious for more? We've got you covered

USA TODAY is exploring the questions you and others ask every day. From "What are the three branches of government?" to "How much does the president make?" to "Is Election Day a federal holiday?" – we're striving to find answers to the most common questions you ask every day. Head to our Just Curious section to see what else we can answer.

search_result.title: In 1791, Augusta played host to George Washington in historic visit
search_result.text:
May 5, 2026 Updated May 12, 2026, 4:42 p.m. ET

Washington's visit in May 1791 was part of his Southern Tour and included parades and celebrations.

He was greeted by local dignitaries and noted the town was well-laid-out in his personal diary.

A long-standing rumor about Washington burying his greyhound in Augusta was later traced to an April Fool's Day article.

George Washington slept here.

At least 17 current orformer U.S. presidents have visited Augusta over its long history, beginning with the nation's first chief executive.

Washington's Southern Tour from March to June 1791 was punctuated by parades, speeches, and other celebrations in which residents at each stop displayed the utmost hospitality.

And Washington recorded it all – some of it, anyway – in his personal diary.

On May 18, 1791, Washington's entourage, continuing from Savannah to Augusta, ate breakfast at an inn on the banks of McBean Creek, "15 miles from Waynesborough," owned by Revolutionary War veteran James Fulcher.

Four miles from Augusta, Georgia's capital at the time, Washington was greeted by a delegation of dignitaries led by Gov. Edward Telfair and George Walton, a signer of the Declaration of Independence.

Washington "was escorted into the Town, and received under a discharge of Artillery," he wrote. A meal and a tea followed at Telfair's Grove plantation, "with many well dressed Ladies."

He also wrote about "between 60 and 70 well dressed ladies" at an assembly at the Academy of Richmond County on May 19. On May 20, he was given a tour of ruined British battlements from the Patriots' recapture of Augusta in 1781.

"The town of Augusta is well laid out with wide and spacious Streets," Washington wrote in his May 20 diary entry. "It stands on a large area of a perfect plain but it is not yet thickly built tho' surprisingly so for the time."

In the decades after Washington's visit, a rumor persisted that while he was in Augusta, the president buried one of his favorite greyhounds, supposedly named Cornwallis after the defeated British general.

"At some time during those three days, President Washington had the sad duty of burying his favorite greyhound," longtime Augusta historian Edward Cashin wrote in his 1980 book "The Story of Augusta."

Longtime Augusta Chronicle columnist pointed out years later that the 1892 newspaper article supposedly documenting the burial was dated April 1 – April Fool's Day.
