search_result.title: Ultimate Midwest road trip: Explore historic and scenic sites
search_result.text:
USA TODAY Network

June 10, 2026 Updated June 18, 2026, 4:32 p.m. ET

As we look back at 250 years of American history, there are many places in the heartland that represent what is now sometimes referred to as "flyover country." But one exploring the historical sites and beauty of the United States would be remiss to fly over the significance of Middle America.

The Midwest is home to the history of sophisticated Indigenous villages and sacred landscapes. It's a place of landmarks – the nation's tallest monument the Gateway Arch in St. Louis, Mount Rushmore and Chimney Rock all marking the "gateway to the West." And edged to the north by the Great Lakes, is a now defining coastal culture with a history that drove industrial innovation, military security and trade.

An exhaustive list might go on, but here we explore a select set of landmarks, museums and travel destinations recommended by local editors to capture a sliver of the historical significance Middle America holds.

This story is part of USA 250 Most Treasured Views, a USA TODAY Network project exploring places across America with historical and cultural significance, created in celebration of the USA 250 initiative marking the nation’s 250th birthday.

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

Illinois

Lincoln Home National Historic Site in Springfield

Abraham Lincoln tourism always means big business in Illinois but especially in Springfield, the site of the Lincoln Home National Historic Site and the only home he ever owned. The homestead was purchased in 1844, and he lived there until moving to the White House in 1861. The Lincoln home site provides another dimension to the study of the man often cited as the nation’s greatest president. (They may be biased in the state commonly referred to as the Land of Lincoln.) Part of the National Park Service, the home’s visitor center also houses a bookstore with new and hard-to-find Lincoln titles, videos and related memorabilia. Plan your visit: nps.gov/liho.

Route 66

Also called The Mother Road, Route 66 stretches almost 2,500 miles and spans eight states, beginning in Chicago and ending in Santa Monica, California. Established in 1926 as a way to connect isolated, rural communities, Route 66 captures a nostalgic era of Americana, one filled with neon signs, diners and classic cars. The iconic highway marks its centennial this year. Road trippers can make pit stops at historic sites and quirky landmarks, like the 66-foot-tall soda bottle at Pops 66 Soda Ranch in Oklahoma. — Kathleen Wong, USA TODAY

Indiana

Clifty Falls State Park

Clifty Falls State Park is the keeper of some of Indiana's ancient history, including 425 million-year-old shale and limestone rocks and fossil remnants that give clues to a former marine ecosystem with corals and ancestral squids. However, public fossil collecting at the park is not allowed. The park is a stop on the Indiana Birding Trail, offering chances to see a diverse array of species at different stages of migration, mating and raising their young. Plan your visit:on.IN.gov/cliftyfallssp. — Domenica Bongiovanni, Indianapolis Star

Discover more: Ancient fossil beds and stunning waterfalls await at this Indiana gem

Iowa

Effigy Mounds National Monument

Effigy Mounds honors the artistry, engineering and spiritual traditions of Indigenous communities who shaped these hills centuries before the United States existed, reminding us – especially as the nation marks 250 years – that America’s story stretches far earlier than 1776. Visitors today can walk wooded trails to the Great Bear Mound and soaring bird effigies perched above the Mississippi River, where celestial alignments and earthwork forms preserve cultural memory and resilience. Interpretive exhibits, wind in the oak forests and eagles drifting below reveal the site’s enduring significance, while rangers and tribal partners share how these mounds remain living heritage, not relics. Plan your visit: nps.gov/efmo. — Susan Stapleton, Des Moines Register

Kansas

Eisenhower Presidential Library, Museum and Boyhood Home

Decades after Americans declared “I like Ike,” tourists love the Kansas attraction that preserves the legacy of President Dwight D. Eisenhower. The site is on a 22-acre campus in the former cowtown of Abilene. It educates visitors about the life and times of Eisenhower, a five-star Army general who was president from 1953 to 1961. Eisenhower museum exhibits show how he grew up in Abilene and rose to become supreme commander of Allied Forces in World War II. Plan your visit:eisenhowerlibrary.gov/visit/visit-us. — Tim Hrenchir, Topeka Capital-Journal

Michigan

Ford Piquette Plant in Detroit

The Ford Piquette Avenue Plant in Detroit, which saw the birth of the Ford Model T, is a significant site in automotive history. It's now home to a museum with more than 65 rare vehicles where visitors can ride in a crate elevator from 1926 and see Henry Ford's old office. The building is on the U.S. National Register of Historic Places and is a U.S. national historic landmark. Plan your visit: fordpiquetteplant.org. — Tanya Wildt, Detroit Free Press

Discover more: Dozens of rare vehicles are inside historic Ford plant in Detroit

Grand Hotel on Mackinac Island

The Grand Hotel opened July 10, 1887, on the northern Michigan island where automobiles are banned, horse-drawn carriages line the streets and visitors flock each summer. Its porch alone is famous. At 660 feet, it is widely billed as the world’s longest and is visible as you approach Mackinac Island from Lake Huron. Visitors can also play golf on The Jewel course, which is the only golf course in the United States with horse-drawn carriage rides between nines. Plan your visit: grandhotel.com. — Tanya Wildt, Detroit Free Press

The Guardian Building in Detroit

The Guardian Building is one of the most recognizable skyscrapers in Detroit's downtown skyline. Located in the city's Financial District, the art deco building was constructed in 1928 and originally called the Union Trust Building. It's known for its tangerine-colored bricks, craftsmanship and blending of Native American, Aztec and Arts and Crafts influences. The building features murals, intricate tile work, mosaic and stained glass, marble fixtures and a vaulted lobby, and is designated a national historic landmark. Plan your visit: guardianbuilding.com. — Tanya Wildt, Detroit Free Press

Discover more: Guardian Building in Detroit has masterful detail and craftsmanship

SS Badger steamship in Ludington

The SS Badger steamship is a National Historic Landmark that isn't on land. The last coal-fired passenger steamship serves as a Lake Michigan ferry that transports passengers, vehicles and commercial trucks between Ludington and Manitowoc, Wisconsin, according to the ship's website. The ferry runs May 15 to Oct. 11. Passengers can also play bingo and board games, watch free movies, lounge indoors and out, visit the open deck for walking and fresh air, and shop in the "Boatique." Plan your visit: ssbadger.com. −Tanya Wildt, Detroit Free Press

Minnesota

Historic Fort Snelling in Minneapolis

Historic Fort Snelling in Minneapolis is a place of strength, heritage and strife. Directly at the confluence of the Mississippi and Minnesota rivers, the location had been home to the Dakota people. The site later became Fort Snelling after the U.S. government acquired the land through treaties with Indigenous nations. The tapestry of its history is broad, weaving together enslaved people, Native people, soldiers, World War II language interpreters and tradespeople. With more than 10,000 years of history behind it, Fort Snelling remains open, serving as a testament to interconnectedness and the nation's history. Fort Snelling State Park is next to the museum. Along with 18 miles of hiking trails, there are five miles of bike trails. There are also locations to rent snowshoes and canoes, and places to swim, picnic and ride a boat. Fort Snelling was the first Minnesota National Treasure, Historic Landmark and Site of Conscience. Plan your visit: nps.gov/miss/planyourvisit/histfort.htm. — A.J. Kaufman, Special to Watertown Public Opinion

Missouri

Gateway Arch in St. Louis

Dubbed the "Gateway to the West," the Gateway Arch in St. Louis is the tallest monument in the United States. The national park spans 91 acres and includes an underground museum detailing the history and architectural marvel of the arch. A tram takes people up 63 stories to the top of the arch, offering views overlooking St. Louis and the Mississippi River. Spring and fall give the best opportunity to enjoy the riverfront and national park grounds without the heat of peak summer. Plan your visit gatewayarch.com. — Marta Mieze, Springfield News-Leader

George Washington Carver National Monument in Diamond

The George Washington Carver National Monument in Diamond, Missouri, is the first National Park Service unit to be dedicated to a Black person. Its namesake, who revolutionized farming in the American South, was born to enslaved parents Moses and Susan Carver near the end of the Civil War. He spent his childhood on the farm exploring the woods and learning, earning him the nickname "the plant doctor." Visitors today can see a reconstructed 14-by-14-foot cabin marking his birthplace. The park is open to the public without a fee nearly every day of the year. Plan your visit: nps.gov/gwca. — Susan Szuch, Springfield News-Leader

National WWI Museum and Memorial in Kansas City

The National WWI Museum and Memorial in Kansas City, Missouri, aims to help today's citizens remember and understand the Great War and how it impacted the global community. The memorial predates the museum and was dedicated in 1926 by President Calvin Coolidge. It was closed in 1994 due to deterioration, and Kansas City residents passed a limited-run sales tax to support the building's restoration in 1998. The memorial and the museum, designated as the nation's official World War I museum by Congress in 2004, host more than 1 million visitors annually. Plan your visit: theworldwar.org. — Susan Szuch, Springfield News-Leader

Nebraska

Chimney Rock

Chimney Rock towers over its surroundings, standing more than 300 feet above the flatlands of western Nebraska. Visible for miles, it served as a significant landmark for emigrants during the westward expansion of the United States, and it is mentioned in more emigrant diaries than any other landmark on the Oregon Trail. It was designated a national historic site on Aug. 9, 1956. Today, there is a visitor center nearby, along with miles of walking trails. They're open to the public at no charge from dawn to dusk, but be sure to stay on the trails to avoid disturbing the rocks, vegetation and wildlife. Plan your visit: history.nebraska.gov/rock. — Chris Mueller, Milwaukee Journal Sentinel

Scotts Bluff in Gering

Scotts Bluff towers about 800 feet above the North Platte River in western Nebraska. It has long served as a landmark for many people who traveled through the area, from Native American tribes to emigrants on the Oregon, California and Mormon trails, according to the National Park Service. In 1919, the Scotts Bluff National Monument was established by presidential proclamation, preserving more than 3,000 acres of land deemed important for both its geological features and human history. Today, visitors can hike miles of trails and take a scenic drive through the bluffs. Plan your visit: nps.gov/scbl/index.htm − Chris Mueller, Milwaukee Journal Sentinel

Durham Western Heritage Museum in Omaha

The Durham Museum is in Omaha's historic Union Station, built by Union Pacific Railroad and opened in 1931. The building is one of the country's best examples of art deco architecture. The museum houses a range of permanent exhibits, including restored train cars, vintage storefronts and other artifacts that highlight the region's history. Plan your visit: durhammuseum.org. — Chris Mueller, Milwaukee Journal Sentinel

North Dakota

National historic site where the Knife River meets the Missouri River

In 1974, Congress established a national historic site where the Knife River meets the Missouri River in central North Dakota. For centuries, Native American tribes lived in the area, growing crops and building earthlodges – circular homes of earth and wood that were the center of family life, housing multiple generations, according to the National Park Service. Now, round depressions in the ground "only hint at the vibrant cultures that once flourished here," the Park Service stated. A replica of an earthlodge, 40 feet in diameter and built using traditional materials, stands next to the historic site's visitor center. Plan your visit: nps.gov/knri/index.htm. — Chris Mueller, Milwaukee Journal Sentinel

Discover more: Knife River historic site was once home to thriving Native American villages

Ronald Reagan Minuteman Missile State Historic Site

The Ronald Reagan Minuteman Missile State Historic Site played a significant role in the United States’ strategy of nuclear deterrence during the Cold War era. It actually consists of two sites, the Oscar-Zero Missile Alert Facility and the November-33 Launch Facility, which are the "last remnants" of the 321st Missile Wing, a collection of intercontinental ballistic missile launch sites scattered across a 6,500-square-mile area around Grand Forks Air Force Base, according to the State Historical Society of North Dakota. Plan your visit: history.nd.gov/historicsites/minutemanmissile/index.html. — Chris Mueller, Milwaukee Journal Sentinel

Discover more: This historic missile site once put North Dakota on the Cold War front lines

Ohio

Marblehead Lighthouse on Lake Erie

Casting its light for more than 200 years, Lake Erie's oldest lighthouse continues to fascinate at Marblehead. The lighthouse is considered to be the best-known and most-photographed landmark on Lake Erie. Visitors can walk the winding 77 steps to the top. A museum and restored buildings bring that history to life. Plan your visit: ohiodnr.gov/go-and-do/plan-a-visit/find-a-property/marblehead-lighthouse-state-park. — Roger LaPointe, Port Clinton News Herald

Discover more: Marblehead Lighthouse beams for more than 200 years

First Ladies Library & Museum in Canton

Museums and libraries dedicated to the nation's presidents dot America. But the country's only library and museum examining the impact of first ladies in the White House is in downtown Canton. Everything first lady occupies multiple floors, including a small theater showing films, and rotating and themed exhibits exploring the influence and legacies of presidential counterparts throughout American history. The items include lavish gowns, magazine covers, books, letters and keepsakes. Plan your visit: firstladies.org. — Ed Balint, Canton Repository

Perry's Victory and International Peace Memorial

Perry’s Victory and International Peace Memorial rises 352 feet above Lake Erie, honoring those who fought in the War of 1812's Battle of Lake Erie and the long-standing peace among the United States, Canada and Britain. Plan your visit: nps.gov/pevi/index.htm. — Roger LaPointe, Port Clinton News Herald

Two Halls of Fame: Pro Football and Rock & Roll

The origins of the NFL can be traced to Canton, Ohio, where the Pro Football Hall of Fame chronicles the history of America's most popular sport. Walk through a gallery of bronze busts immortalizing the game's greatest players and learn about the evolution of the game from the days of leather helmets to modern gear. Plan your visit: profootballhof.com. And while you're feeling nostalgic, an hour drive to Cleveland will treat you to the Rock & Roll Hall of Fame, an immersive exploration of modern music and culture, including artifacts, a chronicle of the evolution of music and exhibits on defining artists. Plan your visit: rockhall.com. — Ed Balint, Canton Repository

Amish country

With a simplistic lifestyle, homegrown traditions and beautiful scenery, Ohio's Amish country provides a unique insight into a specific American history and heritage. Ohio's Amish country is a haven for the simple lifestyle that still practices cultural freedom. From agricultural legacy, alternative modernization and pride in heritage, a lifestyle of independence and historical significance can be witnessed. Ohio town hubs to visit include Millersburg, Berlin and Sugarcreek. Plan your visit: visitamishcountry.com. — Emily Rohozen, Columbus Dispatch

South Dakota

Mount Rushmore and Black Hills National Forest

Mount Rushmore National Memorial, the “Symbol of America,” attracts more than 2 million visitors a year, but it is not the only attraction in the Black Hills near Keystone, South Dakota. Visitors can witness year-round lighting ceremonies and stroll the half-mile Presidential Trail just below the granite busts of George Washington, Thomas Jefferson, Abraham Lincoln and Theodore Roosevelt. While you're in the area, be sure to visit Badlands National Park for its dramatic landscape and fossil history as well as Crazy Horse Memorial, an ongoing Native American monument project. Plan your visit: nps.gov/moru/index.htm. — Angela George, Sioux Falls Argus Leader

Discover more: See why Mount Rushmore is a bucket list trip for families nationwide

Palisades State Park

Palisades State Park in South Dakota is known for its towering Sioux quartzite rock formations, sparkly waterfalls and family-friendly trails. Also visit Devil’s Gulch within the park to learn about the legend of outlaw Jesse James, who allegedly jumped an 18-foot gap on horseback after a botched bank robbery in 1876. The park itself is an enchanting mystery, sudden spires broken through the prairie along state lines and creating what was once known by Native Americans as Spirit Canyon. Plan your visit: gfp.sd.gov/parks/detail/palisades-state-park. — Angela George, Sioux Falls Argus Leader

Wisconsin

Cana Island Lighthouse in Door County

Cana Island Lighthouse offers one of the most incomparable views of Lake Michigan on the Door County peninsula. The 89-foot white tower is perhaps the most famous of Door County's 11 historic lighthouses. Peeking out over its tree-lined island, the lighthouse has guided vessels on the Great Lakes for more than 150 years. Today, the lighthouse is an official county park and open to visitors several months of the year. Plan your visit: dcmm.org/cana-island-lighthouse. — Maia Pandey, Milwaukee Journal Sentinel

Discover more: Cana Island Lighthouse has been a beacon on Lake Michigan since 1869

Milwaukee breweries

Milwaukee isn't called the "beer capital of the world" for nothing. Pabst, Miller Lite, Schlitz and Blatz are just a few of the iconic brewing companies that call Milwaukee – also often dubbed the Brew City – home. But brewing has been an essential industry in Wisconsin for hundreds of years, according to the Wisconsin Historical Society. The industry kickstarted in Wisconsin in the 1830s, and the state had nearly 160 breweries by the Civil War, the organization states. Not only is beer making important to the history of the Badger State, but so is beer drinking. The beverage reigns as a staple for communities and events to this day. Plan your visit: lakefrontbrewery.com/brewery-tours/our-famous-tour. — Cailey Gleeson, Milwaukee Journal Sentinel

Wisconsin Dells

When you think of the Wisconsin Dells, you probably think of indoor waterparks. And rightfully so, since it's the birthplace of the modern industry. The first indoor waterpark in the United States was established in the Dells in 1989. But the area has more to offer than just waterpark tourism. From a scenic walk along the Wisconsin River or nearby state parks, take in the views on one of the countless hiking trails around the Dells. Or hop on the river, which offers kayaking, boat tours and more. Plan your visit: wisdells.com. — Cailey Gleeson, Milwaukee Journal Sentinel

Explore more amazing locations that helped define American history with USA 250 Most Treasured Views.

search_result.title: Your complete guide to America's 250th birthday in Louisville
search_result.text:
June 4, 2026 Updated June 18, 2026, 11:47 a.m. ET

Louisville will host a "Thunder-style" fireworks show at Waterfront Park for America's 250th anniversary.

The city's celebration includes a communal breakfast, patriotic performances, and free museum admission.

Other festivities include concerts, festivals, and events at Bernheim Forest and the Kentucky State Capitol.

Kentucky is home to several iconic American brands and landmarks, including the Kentucky Derby and Louisville Slugger.

Louisville has a reputation for throwing a big party, and the 250th anniversary of the signing of the Declaration of Independence is no exception.

As the home of the annual largest firework show in the country, the Kentucky Derby Festival's Thunder Over Louisville, the city has promised a “Thunder-style” Fourth of July celebration at its beloved Waterfront Park, among other activities to honor America 250. The celebration will be the biggest in 30 years, according to Louisville Mayor Craig Greenberg.

While that show will certainly headline Louisville’s America 250 celebration, there are many ways to celebrate the birth of the nation and Kentucky’s legacy and contributions to the United States’ 250-year story.

More July 4 news: 'Thunder-style' fireworks, special performances planned for America 250 in Louisville

Looking to celebrate America 250 in Louisville? Here’s everything you need to know about these once in a lifetime festivities.

What makes this July 4 different in Louisville?

America is celebrating its 250 anniversary in 2026, which means everything around July 4 in Louisville is bigger and better. Thunder-level fireworks at Waterfront Park are certainly a major attraction this Fourth of July, but Louisville’s America 250 committee has several events on the books that make this Independence Day stand out from years’ past.

Whether you’re looking to quietly reflect, sing and dance, or cheer the city of Louisville has a few more ways than usual to mark the day.

Locust Grove, 561 Blankenbaker Lane, which is among the oldest historic homes in the state, is hosting a free communal breakfast on July 4 from 6:30 a.m. to 10 a.m. with silent reflection around a sacred fire as well as a multicultural ceremony with dance and music.

Later that afternoon at Metro Hall, at 527 W. Jefferson St., the public can enjoy a series of patriotic performances that honor Paul Revere’s historic ride and the Declaration of Independence. Select cast members of the Broadway Across America touring company of "Hamilton" will also perform for the crowd.

The Frazier History Museum at 829 W. Main St. is offering free admission from 1-4 p.m. to kick off the opening of its "I Too Am a Kentuckian" exhibition.

Explore and celebrate America: Subscribe to our weekly USA 250 newsletter

Wayne Hettinger, who has produced Thunder Over Louisville since its inception in 1990, has been working on the America 250 firework display for more than six months. Activities at Waterfront Park’s Great Lawn will begin at 5 p.m. on Independence Day and culminate with evening of family activities, festival food and Brown-Forman bars alongside a live music lineup that highlights the richness and evolution of American music.

Where to celebrate July 4 in Louisville

In addition to the events above, including the massive firework show and Independence Day event at Waterfront Park’s Great Lawn in downtown Louisville, here's what else you can do to celebrate:

Know an event we missed? Send a note to Gege Reed at greed@usatoday.com.com.

July 2

The Louisville Orchestra "Play America,"America Place ,101 Logistics Ave., in Jeffersonville, Indiana: Music director Teddy Abrams will fire up the holiday spirit with its fourth annual Play America concert. The family-friendly event kicks off at 6 p.m. with an expanded selection of food trucks, additional community musical performances, and a broader range of activities including balloon art, inflatables, and face painting. At 8:30 p.m., Abrams will lead the orchestra in a high‑energy program that swings from Bernstein’s "Candide Overture" and Dvořák’s "Symphony No. 8" to Vieuxtemps’ "Souvenir d’Amérique," with violin soloists. Admission is free, but registration is encouraged. Guests should bring their own seating. Parking, food, and beverages will be available on‑site. For more details and registration, visit louisvilleorchestra.org/events/detail/play-america.

July 3

J-Town Stars and Stripes Fest,10721 Old Taylorsville Road: Celebrate Independence Day weekend at the first-ever J-Town Stars & Stripes Fest at the Jeffersontown Amphitheater featuring country music star JD Shelburne live in concert. Kick off the holiday weekend with a fun, family-friendly night of live music under the summer sky. 6 p.m. Tickets: $10. jtownamphitheater.com/events

Summer Concert Guide: There's a summer concert in Louisville for every budget in 2026

July 4

Louisville July 4 Celebration, multiple locations: Louisville Mayor Craig Greenberg announced three events for the city's Jule 4 celebration, including a communal breakfast at Locust Grove, "patriotic performances" at Metro Hall – including some by the touring cast of "Hamilton" – and a free, family-friendly event at Waterfront Park. The celebration will be the biggest in more than 30 years, Greenberg said, and will conclude with a firework show produced by Wayne Hettinger, the longtime producer of Thunder Over Louisville. Find more details here.

July 4 at the Capitol, 700 Central Ave., Frankfort: The Kentucky Sestercentennial Commission in partnership with the City of Frankfort and Franklin County will host a statewide televised event featuring nationally recognized entertainers, a grammy award winning symphonic orchestra, and Kentucky’s Poet Laureate at the Kentucky State Capitol. With dignitaries and citizens alike, the event will commemorate this remarkable achievement and examine the influence the Declaration of Independence. Free. america250.org/event/july-4-at-the-capitol

2026 Crescent Hill 4th of July Festival, 301 St. Peterson Ave.: Celebrate America's 250th birthday at one of Louisville's most beloved traditions on the grounds of the historic Peterson Dumesnil House, 301 South Peterson Ave. This free, family-friendly event features a full day of activities and entertainment, including an art fair, food trucks, craft beer, live music, a cake wheel, raffle and silent auction, pet contest, cake baking contest, magician, children's inflatable Fun Zone and more. 10 a.m. to 10 p.m. crescenthill.us/4th-of-july-event

America: Fourth of July Celebration at Bernheim Forest and Arboretum, 2075 Clermont Road, Clermont: Celebrate Independence Day with classic family fun at Bernheim. Gather with loved ones for a picnic and enjoy an afternoon of outdoor games, activities, and shared time in the beauty of the forest. 11 a.m. to 3 p.m.

J-Town Stars and Stripes Fest,10721 Old Taylorsville Road: Celebrate Independence Day weekend at the first-ever J-Town Stars & Stripes Fest at the Jeffersontown Amphitheater featuring The Crashers live in concert. Kick off the holiday weekend with a fun, family-friendly night of live music under the summer sky. 6 p.m. Tickets: $10. jtownamphitheater.com/events

July Fourth celebrations across Kentucky

Bowling Green, Ky.

WHAT: Numerous events are taking place in Bowling Green the weekend of July Fourth:

July 2, 7:30 p.m.: "A Star-Spangled Spectacular: Celebrating 250 Years of America." Concert by Orchestra Kentucky and the Orchestra Kentucky Chorale with Steve Amerson, guest vocalist. Free. The Southern Kentucky Performing Arts Center, 601 College St. Free, reservations are required. theskypac.com/event/a-star-spangled-spectacular-celebrating-250-years-of-america-free-concert.

"A Star-Spangled Spectacular: Celebrating 250 Years of America." Concert by Orchestra Kentucky and the Orchestra Kentucky Chorale with Steve Amerson, guest vocalist. Free. The Southern Kentucky Performing Arts Center, 601 College St. Free, reservations are required. theskypac.com/event/a-star-spangled-spectacular-celebrating-250-years-of-america-free-concert. July 3, 4 p.m.: Hayward Minton Thunderfest Fireworks Show and Music Festival. Enjoy family friendly activities, food trucks, vendors, kids zone, local and regional musicians throughout the day, and concert by Gracee Shriver, ending with fireworks. Ephram White Park, 885 Mt. Olivet Road. $25 per car load.

Hayward Minton Thunderfest Fireworks Show and Music Festival. Enjoy family friendly activities, food trucks, vendors, kids zone, local and regional musicians throughout the day, and concert by Gracee Shriver, ending with fireworks. Ephram White Park, 885 Mt. Olivet Road. $25 per car load. July 4: Celebrate the 4th of July at Beach Bend Park. Experience Beech Bend Park after dark. Rides come alive with dazzling LED lights, transforming the park into a glowing wonderland. Admission includes unlimited access to all rides from 4:30-9 p.m. and entry to Splash Lagoon Water Park from 4:30-6:30 p.m. Tickets start at $36.99. Beach Bend Park, 798 Beech Bend Road. beechbend.com/amusement-park.

Celebrate the 4th of July at Beach Bend Park. Experience Beech Bend Park after dark. Rides come alive with dazzling LED lights, transforming the park into a glowing wonderland. Admission includes unlimited access to all rides from 4:30-9 p.m. and entry to Splash Lagoon Water Park from 4:30-6:30 p.m. Tickets start at $36.99. Beach Bend Park, 798 Beech Bend Road. beechbend.com/amusement-park. July 4, 6 p.m.: Bowling Green Hot Rods 4th of July Spectacular. The Hot Rods are the city’s Minor League Baseball team. They are not playing but enjoy live music from The Spazmatics and a DJ, hot dog eating contest, carnival games, a large fireworks show and more. Admission starts at $16.50.

Bowling Green Hot Rods 4th of July Spectacular. The Hot Rods are the city’s Minor League Baseball team. They are not playing but enjoy live music from The Spazmatics and a DJ, hot dog eating contest, carnival games, a large fireworks show and more. Admission starts at $16.50. July 4, 9 p.m.: Friends of Barren River Lake 4th of July Fireworks Show. Barren River Lake State Resort Park will have fireworks. Watch from hillside, tennis courts or from the water. 1149 State Park Road, Lucas, Kentucky.

WHERE: Various locations

WHEN: July 2-4

MORE INFORMATION:visitbgky.com/blog/post/where-to-celebrate-americas-250-in-bowling-green-kentucky

You may like: Your complete guide to America’s 250th birthday in Louisville

Fort Knox, Ky.

WHAT: Celebrate America 250th Birthday at Freedom Fest with live music, K-9 demonstration by Fort Knox’s 905th Military Working Dog Detachment, parade of state and territorial flags with the firing of 51 artillery cannon rounds, kids zone and fireworks (10 p.m.).

Schedule:

6-8 p.m.: Inflatables

Inflatables 6-9 p.m.: Kids activities and tattoos

Kids activities and tattoos 7-10 p.m.: Live Music

Live Music 7:30-8 p.m.: Military Working Dog Demonstration

Military Working Dog Demonstration 9-9:30 p.m.: Salute to the Nation Ceremony

Salute to the Nation Ceremony 10 p.m.: Fireworks

WHERE: Brooks Field, Old Ironside Ave., Fort Knox

WHEN: July 4, 6-11 p.m.

MORE INFORMATION:militaryspot.com/news/celebrate-america-250-with-july-4-freedom-fest.

Frankfort, Ky.

WHAT: Celebrate America's 250 birthday in Frankfort with an American 250 Music Festival (July 3) and Kentucky Celebrates America250 (July 4).

July 3, 2-10 p.m.: Liberty Hall-er America 250 Music Festival. Liberty Hall Historic Site, Ward Oates Amphitheater, Riverview Park, 404 Wilkinson Blvd. Performers include Cole Chaney, The Local Honeys, Hancock and Shouse, Mash Grass, Violet & the Newsroom, Jeri & Nat. Also activities, food trucks and vendors. Free.

Liberty Hall-er America 250 Music Festival. Liberty Hall Historic Site, Ward Oates Amphitheater, Riverview Park, 404 Wilkinson Blvd. Performers include Cole Chaney, The Local Honeys, Hancock and Shouse, Mash Grass, Violet & the Newsroom, Jeri & Nat. Also activities, food trucks and vendors. Free. July 4, 10 a.m. to 10 p.m.: Kentucky Celebrates America250. Kentucky Historical Society Campus, 100 W. Broadway St. A day of free family fun in historic downtown Frankfort, explore Kentucky's past with genealogy workshops; stop by the Keeneland galleries for a talk on Daniel Boone; children's activities (games, giant checkers, Jenga, and cornhole) and free hot dogs while supplies last at the the Cralle Day Garden; sign a replica of the Declaration of Independence with quill and ink and end the day at the Old State Capitol grounds with music from Kentucky Blue, Walker Montgomery, The Louisville Orchestra, Dr. Everett McCorvey will sing the national snthem and "America the Beautiful," and Ben Sollee will perform "My Old Kentucky Home." Gov. Andy Beshear will give welcoming remarks, followed by orchestral selections and a grand fireworks finale set to music.

WHERE: Kentucky Historical Society Campus, 100 W Broadway St., Frankfort

WHEN: July 3, 2-10 p.m.; July, 10 a.m. to 10 p.m.

MORE INFORMATION:history.ky.gov/events/kentucky-celebrates-america250.

Hodgenville, Ky.

WHAT: Hodgenville's tribute to America250 will be held in Creekfront Park. This family-friendly event features numerous free activities including bounce houses, playgrounds and games for the young and young at heart along with lively musical performances from the bandstand. Food vendors, face painting and fireworks (9:30 p.m.).

WHERE: Hodgenville Creekfront Park, 250 Tonieville Road, Hodgenville

WHEN: July 4, 4-10 p.m.

CST: Free

MORE INFORMATION: america250.org/event/4th-of-july-celebrate-freedom/

You may like: 3 of Kentucky's most beautiful places to visit for a summer road trip

Lexington, Ky.

WHAT: Lexington will celebrate America's 250th birthday with events from June 26 through July 5

June 26, 7 p.m.: Friday Flicks: "Hamilton" Broadway movie. Jacobson Park, 4001 Athens-Boonesboro Road. Activities, 7 p.m.; movie, dark.

Friday Flicks: "Hamilton" Broadway movie. Jacobson Park, 4001 Athens-Boonesboro Road. Activities, 7 p.m.; movie, dark. June 27, 11 a.m. to 4 p.m.: Bluegrass 10,000 Wellness Expo, Phoenix Park, 100 E. Main St. and Pam Miller Downtown Arts Center, 141 E. Main St.

Bluegrass 10,000 Wellness Expo, Phoenix Park, 100 E. Main St. and Pam Miller Downtown Arts Center, 141 E. Main St. June 30 , 7-8:30 p.m.: Big Band and Jazz: Patriotic Night. Featuring the Lexington Summer Concert Band. Moondance Amphitheater, 1152 Monarch St.

, Big Band and Jazz: Patriotic Night. Featuring the Lexington Summer Concert Band. Moondance Amphitheater, 1152 Monarch St. July 1, 5:30-8 p.m.: America 250 Birthday Party & Ice Cream Social. Fifth Third Bank Pavilion, Tandy Park, 251 W. Main St.

America 250 Birthday Party & Ice Cream Social. Fifth Third Bank Pavilion, Tandy Park, 251 W. Main St. July 2, 7 p.m.: Southland Jamboree: Patriotic Edition. Featuring Six String Soldiers U.S. Army Band. Moondance Amphitheater, 1152 Monarch St.

Southland Jamboree: Patriotic Edition. Featuring Six String Soldiers U.S. Army Band. Moondance Amphitheater, 1152 Monarch St. July 3, 5-9 p.m.: Patriotic Concert. Transylvania University and Gratz Park, 250 W. Third St. Pre-show, 5 p.m. with Young at Heart; main show, 7:30 p.m. with Lexington Philharmonic.

Patriotic Concert. Transylvania University and Gratz Park, 250 W. Third St. Pre-show, 5 p.m. with Young at Heart; main show, 7:30 p.m. with Lexington Philharmonic. July 4 , 7:30 a.m. to 10 p.m.: 50th Annual Bluegrass 10,000, 5K and Fun Run. Corner of Main St. and N. Limestone St. (7:25 a.m.); Fourth of July Festival & Market. Robert F. Stephens Courthouse Plaza, 120 N. Limestone St. and Fifth Third Bank Pavilion, Tandy Park, 251 W. Main St. (10 a.m. to 3 p.m.); Public Reading of the United States Declaration of Independence, Lexington Courthouse, 215 W. Main St. (10 a.m.); Fourth of July Parade. Main St. (downtown from Midland Ave. to N. Mill St.) noon; Fourth of July Fireworks Spectacular. Launched from the R. J. Corman rail yard, near intersection of Main St. and Oliver Lewis Way (10 p.m.).

, 50th Annual Bluegrass 10,000, 5K and Fun Run. Corner of Main St. and N. Limestone St. (7:25 a.m.); Fourth of July Festival & Market. Robert F. Stephens Courthouse Plaza, 120 N. Limestone St. and Fifth Third Bank Pavilion, Tandy Park, 251 W. Main St. (10 a.m. to 3 p.m.); Public Reading of the United States Declaration of Independence, Lexington Courthouse, 215 W. Main St. (10 a.m.); Fourth of July Parade. Main St. (downtown from Midland Ave. to N. Mill St.) noon; Fourth of July Fireworks Spectacular. Launched from the R. J. Corman rail yard, near intersection of Main St. and Oliver Lewis Way (10 p.m.). July 5, 7-9 p.m.: Summer Nights: Red, White & Blues with Tee Dee Young. Moondance Amphitheater, 1152 Monarch St.

MORE INFORMATION:lexingtonky.gov/news/red-white-blue-250-years-america-lexington-plans-star-spangled-fourth-july-celebration

Shelbyville, Ky.

WHAT: The July Fourth celebration starts with the Shelbyville Independence Day Parade on Main Street (leaving from the Shelby County Fairgrounds at 10 a.m.) and proceeding down Main Street; following the parade head to the Shelby County Historical Society Museum for a dramatic reading of the Declaration of Independence, and the Daughters of the American Revolution will lead the ringing bells for a nationwide movement known as Freedom Bells at noon.

WHERE: 627 Main St., Shelbyville

WHEN: July 4, 10 a.m.

MORE INFORMATION: kentuckyliving.com/event/july-4th-parade-historical-celebration.

You may like: Here's how you can tour the Chevrolet Corvette plant in Kentucky

Simpsonville, Ky.

WHAT: Outlet Shoppes of the Bluegrass will host the Fourth of July weekend celebration in honor of America's 250th birthday. Featuring living history experiences, Independence Market, live patriotic presentations, interactive entertainment, character appearances and ending with Simpsonville’s The Boom (fireworks, July 4, 10 p.m.) at Corner of Buck Creek Road and US 60.

WHERE: The Outlet Shoppes of the Bluegrass, I-64, exit 28, Simpsonville, Ky.

WHEN: July 3-6

COST: Free

MORE INFORMATION: Parking is located along US 60, Simpsonville Park and Wiche Park. For a complete schedule of events go to, theoutletshoppesofthebluegrass.com.

What makes celebrating July 4 in Louisville uniquely American?

Kentucky is the kind of place where you can tour a world-renowned urban bourbon district in Louisville, cross a naturally carved bridge or explore the longest known cave system in the world. The commonwealth was the birthplace and childhood home of the 16th President of the United States Abraham Lincoln and “The Greatest” internationally celebrated boxer Muhammad Ali.

Louisville is also home to the Kentucky Derby, which is the oldest continuously running sporting event in the country. Churchill Downs, the storied track where The Run for the Roses is held each year, first opened in 1875. In the 150-plus years that have followed, the track at 700 Central Ave. has welcomed some of the most impressive athletes – both human and horses – in our nation’s history. Eddie Arcaro and Bill Hartack, who both have five Kentucky Derby wins, share the record for the most victories. Secretariat set the record for the fastest Kentucky Derby run in 1973 at 1:59:40, and that seemingly unbeatable time still holds more than five decades later.

Louisville Slugger Museum and Factory at 800 W. Main St. also plays a key role in America’s favorite pastime, baseball. The historic factory first began crafting baseball bats in 1884, and the family-owned company has crafted bats for some of the greatest players of all time such as Babe Ruth, Hank Aaron, Willie Mays and Ken Griffey, Jr. The company makes more than 1.8 million bats of all sizes each year, and most major league baseball players order an average of 120 bats per season. The museum and factory is open year round for tours.

Plan your July 4 in Louisville: Weather, fireworks and more

Pre-planning your July 4 adventure? Here's what to know about the early forecast, if fireworks are legal and more.

July 4 weather forecast

While it's a little too early to predict what weather Kentucky will see on Saturday, July 4, the average monthly temperature in July is 89 degrees, with a low 68 degrees. Read more about what to expect from July weather in Kentucky at kentuckytourism.com/trip-planning/travel-tools/weather.

Are fireworks legal in Kentucky for the Fourth of July? How to use them safely

Yes. According to the Kentucky State Fire Marshal, fireworks are allowed in the state when following laws as listed below.

Use fireworks outdoors.

Obey local laws.

Keep a bucket of water or a working water hose nearby.

Use fireworks as intended and avoid altering them or combining them.

Never relight "dud" fireworks. Wait 20 minutes and then soak it in a bucket of water.

Spectators should keep a safe distance from the person lighting the firework (the individual lighting it should wear safety glasses).

Alcohol and fireworks do not mix. Have a "designated adult operator."

Do not use homemade fireworks or illegal explosives.

Report illegal explosives to the fire or police department in your community.

Never allow children to play with or ignite fireworks.

Read and follow all instructions on the label.

Are fireworks legal in Louisville for the Fourth of July?

Yes and no. Explosive fireworks and ones that leave the ground are illegal. Read more here.

America 250 History: Kentucky

One hundred and fifty years ago, as our nation’s founding fathers laid out the groundwork for a new country in 1776, Kentucky was an untamed frontier connected to Virginia.

Another two years would pass, before George Rogers Clark would found what would become the state’s largest city, Louisville, and eventually Kentucky would join the Union as the 15th state in 1972. It was among the first states to join the union after the Revolutionary War ended, second only to Vermont in 1791.

More city history: Beloved Louisville icon could return to public view after 11-year absence

Harrodsburg, which is located about 80 miles southeast of Louisville, became the first permanent European settlement in 1774. Throughout the mid-1770s groups of explorers and pioneers traveled the region. Many indigenous people such as the Chickasaw, Shawnee and Cherokee people lived on the land for more than 10,000 years before white people arrived. Daniel Boone led expeditions through the Cumberland Gap and helped to settle trading posts and the earliest towns, such as Boonesborough and what’s now Maysville in 1775.

The word “Kentucky,” which Europeans originally spelled as “Kentucke,” likely stems from an indigenous word but it’s unclear which one, according to the U.S. Department of the Interior.

Clark claimed the name was derived from the Indian word "Kentake," meaning "meadow land." Other possibilities are that the name stems from the Shawnee word meaning "at the head of a river" or from the Wyandot word "Ken-tah-ten," meaning "land of tomorrow.”

Kentucky was also the site of the first Independence Day celebration west of the Allegheny Mountains, which is a heavily forested section of the Appalachian Mountains. Col. William Price, veteran of the Revolutionary War, hosted a group of 40 veterans at his plantation to the south of Lexington near what’s now Jessamine County to commemorate the "glorious birthday of our freedom."

Kentucky's most iconic beverage: Bourbon

If you’re looking to raise a glass to the United States, perhaps there's no better place to do it in the homeland of America’s native alcoholic spirit. In 1964, the U.S. Congress passed a resolution declaring bourbon whiskey is a "distinctive product of the United States," and today more than 95 percent of the world’s bourbon comes from Kentucky.

Historically, this is because of Kentucky’s limestone-rich water, but as the beverage boomed in popularity throughout much of the past decade, the number of distilleries has grown from a few dozen to more than 100.

Louisville, Bardstown and Lexington are largely considered hubs for bourbon tourism, but the Kentucky Distiller’s Association says there are now distilleries in more than 40 of Kentucky’s 120 counties.

In 2024, the Kentucky Bourbon Trail celebrated 25 years in existence.

“The 25th anniversary of the Kentucky Bourbon Trail is a great opportunity to celebrate our red-hot signature spirits industry and booming tourism sector,” Gov. Andy Beshear said previously, "with an incredible 2.55 million tours given to Kentucky Bourbon Trail visitors last year alone. I want to thank and congratulate the Kentucky Distillers’ Association and everyone who continues to advance bourbon and tourism in the state.”

Read more about the Kentucky Bourbon Trail here.

Most Treasured Views in Kentucky

Kentucky’s vast and diverse terrain is home to several renown natural, national wonders as well as collection of quaint towns and vibrant small and mid-sized cities. From east to west, commonwealth spans more than 400 miles and 40,000 square-miles, and its filled with adventures and history.

Here are a few places within a reasonable drive of Louisville to explore in honor of America 250.

Cumberland Falls State Park: This slice of Southeastern Kentucky in Corbin, Kentucky, which is often referred to as the “Niagara of the South” is the only place in the Western Hemisphere where a moonbow can be predicted with any regularity. The Cumberland Falls face north and flow north, which allow light to reflect off the moon and form a rainbow in the dark.

Falls of the Ohio State Park: The Falls of the Ohio was named for a treacherous two-mile long series of waterfalls on the Ohio River that once impeded river traffic and inevitably caused the nearby communities of Louisville and Portland in Kentucky as well as the Southern Indiana towns of Clarksville, Jeffersonville and New Albany to flourish due to their ports. Today, its an Indiana State Park known as for having the largest naturally exposed Devonian-period fossil beds in the world, which allows visitors walk directly over ancient marine life preserved in the exposed limestone bedrock

Abraham Lincoln Birthplace National Historical Park: This park and museum, which is near Hodgenville about 50 miles south of Louisville, honors the humble beginnings of the 16th president of the United States, Abraham Lincoln. The neoclassical pink granite and marble Memorial Building is home to the “Symbolic Birth Cabin,” which is what a 19th century Kentucky cabin would have looked like when Lincoln was born in 1809. This dynamic tribute was constructed 11 years before the Lincoln Memorial in Washington D.C.

Natural Bridge State Resort Park: Nature has spent millions of years carving out the natural sandstone arch, creating a natural bridge that is 78-feet long and 65 feet high. This “natural bridge” is a focal point of Kentucky’s Natural Bridge State Park near Slade, Kentucky. The park is popular hiking spot, and it’s known for its sky lift, which ferries guest to a stunning view at the top.

Mammoth Cave National Park: Mammoth Cave, which is located about 95 miles southwest of Louisville, is 420 known miles of charted caves in Western Kentucky is the longest known cave system in the world. These caves were formed over millions of years as rainwater dissolved limestone and formed passageways. The first known tour of the caves occurred in 1816, and this system has blossomed into an international travel destination.
